text: He should look exactly like this, have an income exactly like this, be this tall, have exactly this personality trait, and do exactly these things for me. Why are we so convinced that our cautious mind is the highest form of wisdom available to us? What I thought I wanted was completely wrong for me. There were things that I wanted because I thought that they would make me happy, they would make me feel important somehow, maybe admired. I think social media has fully distorted our relationship to desire. A lot of modern-day aspiration and desire is aesthetic. I don't think it's existential. I'm attractive. I am chosen. I am important. I am winning. What if our desires are actually confused? What if they are reactive? What if we are compensating for something? What if they're based in fear? What if they're socially conditioned? What if underneath the desire is a more important desire than the desire itself? I know exactly what I need. I know exactly what I want, who I want to be. I know exactly what success will look like. I know exactly what type of life will be meaningful to me. Do we? Really? Maybe there's a difference between moving towards something and becoming psychologically dependent on obtaining it. Whatever God sees as most fitting for my life, that is what I hope for.
